text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY ALLOGENEIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT CLINIC
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE -- DAY +120 POST-TRANSPLANT

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Transplant Hematology/Oncology]
CO-MANAGING SERVICE: Leukemia Clinic

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Routine post-transplant follow-up at day +120. The patient presents today for her scheduled 4-month assessment. She reports overall gradual improvement but has new complaints of dry eyes, dry mouth, and skin tightening on her forearms over the past 3-4 weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with a history of ELN 2022 Adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio 0.91, NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8, co-mutations in DNMT3A R882H, TET2, WT1, NRAS, KDM6A, STAG2, and BCOR) who achieved MRD-negative complete remission after a single cycle of 7+3 (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin (RATIFY protocol). She subsequently underwent myeloablative conditioning allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell transplantation from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (34-year-old, CMV-seropositive) with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) conditioning and tacrolimus/methotrexate GVHD prophylaxis. She is now at day +120.

Post-transplant course summary:
- Engraftment was achieved on day +20 (neutrophil) and day +24 (platelet).
- Day +30 bone marrow biopsy: morphologic CR, MRD-negative by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR, full donor myeloid chimerism (99.4% overall, 100% CD33+ myeloid).
- Acute GVHD Grade II (Glucksberg) was diagnosed between days +45 and +65, involving skin (Stage 2, ~30% BSA macular-papular rash) and GI tract (Stage 1, Grade 2 diarrhea up to 1 liter/day). Treated with methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day starting day +47, with excellent response. Skin rash cleared within 10 days; GI symptoms resolved by day +65. Steroids were tapered at 10% per week over 6 weeks starting day +55.
- Day +90 surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no evidence of lymphadenopathy, no extramedullary disease, complete resolution of prior hepatosplenomegaly, with a small area of right lower lobe ground-glass opacity felt to be post-infectious or inflammatory -- this was noted to be followed.
- She was discharged from the transplant unit on day +26 and has been followed in outpatient clinic since.

Since her last visit approximately 3-4 weeks ago, Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reports the gradual onset of bilateral dry, gritty eye discomfort. She has been using over-the-counter artificial tears with minimal relief. She also notes decreased salivation and mild difficulty eating dry foods. Additionally, she has noticed subtle skin tightening and dryness along the dorsal forearms bilaterally -- she described the skin as feeling "stiff" when she tries to make a fist. She denies frank pain, open sores, or difficulty breathing. She reports no new rash elsewhere. She denies diarrhea or change in stool frequency or consistency. She denies dysphagia beyond the xerostomia. No fever, no chills. No bleeding. No signs of respiratory compromise. She is eating a full diet and her weight is stable.

Review of recent labs (within last 2 weeks):
- CBC: WBC 5,800/uL, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, platelets 187,000/uL
- LFTs and renal function: within normal limits
- Tacrolimus trough: 7.2 ng/mL (target currently 8-12 ng/mL during taper phase)

Functional status: She has returned to part-time work as a high school biology teacher (approximately 50-60% of her usual schedule). She lives at home with her husband. She reports ongoing mild fatigue with decreased stamina but is managing household activities and daily walks.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
(+) Dry eyes bilaterally (new, 3-4 weeks)
(+) Dry mouth, reduced salivation (new, 3-4 weeks)
(+) Skin tightening/stiffness, bilateral dorsal forearms (new, 3-4 weeks)
(+) Mild fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance compared to pre-diagnosis baseline
(+) Mild bilateral distal toe tingling/numbness (ongoing, previously attributed to tacrolimus -- stable or slightly improved)
(-) No fever, chills, or night sweats
(-) No new skin rash, blistering, or pruritus beyond described xerosis
(-) No diarrhea, rectal bleeding, or nausea
(-) No dysphagia or odynophagia
(-) No cough, dyspnea, or pleuritic chest pain
(-) No joint pain or swelling
(-) No dysuria or hematuria
(-) No headache or neurological symptoms beyond toe paresthesias
(-) No weight change

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. AML, ELN Adverse-risk (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8) -- in CR1, MRD negative, day +120 post-allogenic HSCT
2. Allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell transplantation from 10/10 MUD, myeloablative conditioning (FluBu4), day 0 approximately 4 months ago
3. Acute GVHD Grade II (skin Stage 2, GI Stage 1) -- resolved on steroids
4. Febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (during induction) -- resolved
5. Grade 3 mucositis (peri-transplant) -- resolved
6. Hypothyroidism -- on levothyroxine, stable
7. Mild hypertension -- previously managed (medications on hold/adjusted peri-transplant)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Married, two adult children. High school biology teacher, returning to part-time work. Non-smoker, no significant alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Lives at home; appropriate caregiver support from husband. No known significant infectious exposures.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Not contributory to AML or hematologic malignancy per prior documentation. No family history of lymphoma or leukemia known.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA (No known drug allergies) per prior records.

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Tacrolimus 0.5 mg orally twice daily (ongoing taper; trough target currently 8-12 ng/mL; plan to taper further if cGVHD remains stable)
2. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800 mg orally once daily (PCP prophylaxis)
3. Acyclovir 400 mg orally twice daily (HSV/VZV prophylaxis)
4. Fluconazole 100 mg orally once daily (antifungal prophylaxis; per protocol through month 6)
5. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (hypothyroidism)
6. Polyethylene glycol artificial tears as needed (initiated by patient OTC)
7. Multivitamin with calcium/vitamin D daily
(Note: Letermovir discontinued at day +100 per protocol; no CMV reactivation to date. Methylprednisolone taper completed.)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8 C, HR 74 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, O2 saturation 98% on room air. Weight 64.2 kg (stable from prior visit).

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing, no acute distress. Appears slightly fatigued but comfortable.

HEENT:
- Eyes: Conjunctival injection mild bilaterally, no discharge, no exudate. Decreased tear lake on slit-lamp equivalent examination. Schirmer's test performed: right eye 6 mm/5 minutes, left eye 5 mm/5 minutes -- both below the normal threshold of 10 mm/5 minutes, consistent with aqueous-deficient dry eye/sicca.
- Oral cavity: Mild mucosal erythema of buccal mucosa bilaterally. Reduced salivary pooling in the floor of mouth. No lichenoid plaques, no erosions, no ulcerations. No oral thrush. Teeth and gingiva appear intact. No active bleeding.

Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or decreased breath sounds.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Spleen is not palpable. Liver edge not appreciated below costal margin. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Skin: Bilateral dorsal forearms with subtle xerosis and early skin thickening -- the skin has a slightly tightened, mildly indurated quality on palpation without frank sclerodermatous changes or full skin immobility. No erythematous macular-papular rash. No blistering or erosions. No jaundice, no petechiae. Nails: no nail changes.

Musculoskeletal: Range of motion of wrists and fingers mildly restricted on the right due to forearm skin changes; patient can fully extend fingers but notes slight resistance. No joint effusions, no tenderness.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Bilateral distal lower extremity sensation mildly reduced to light touch in the toes -- stable from prior visit. No motor weakness. Gait normal.

Lymph Nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy appreciated on full exam.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS:
CBC (today): WBC 5,800/uL, ANC 3,420/uL, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, platelets 187,000/uL -- counts appropriately recovered, consistent with full engraftment and ongoing marrow reconstitution.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 98 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 67 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL -- all within normal limits, no hepatic GVHD or tacrolimus-related nephrotoxicity.
Tacrolimus trough: 7.2 ng/mL.
Thyroid function: TSH 2.1 mIU/L (within normal range on stable levothyroxine dose).
CMV PCR (peripheral blood): Not detected.
EBV PCR (peripheral blood): Not detected.
Peripheral blood FLT3-ITD PCR (most recent, 3 weeks ago): Not detected.
Peripheral blood STR chimerism (most recent, 3 weeks ago): Overall chimerism 99.1% donor, CD3+ T-cell 94.8% donor, CD33+ myeloid 100% donor -- continued full engraftment.

Schirmer's test (performed today as noted above): Right 6 mm/5 min, Left 5 mm/5 min.

---

IMAGING:
Day +90 CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (prior): No lymphadenopathy, no extramedullary disease, complete resolution of hepatosplenomegaly; small RLL ground-glass opacity, felt to be post-infectious. This was to be followed on interval imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with ELN Adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 WT, trisomy 8, complex co-mutation profile) who is now at day +120 post-myeloablative 10/10 MUD allo-HSCT, with full donor engraftment, sustained MRD-negative complete remission, and no evidence of leukemic relapse.

She presents today with new findings of bilateral sicca features (dry eyes with abnormal Schirmer's test, reduced salivation), mild oral mucosal erythema, and early skin involvement of the bilateral dorsal forearms with subtle skin thickening -- all of which emerged over the past 3-4 weeks in the context of tapering immunosuppression. Her prior acute GVHD Grade II (skin and GI) was treated successfully with steroids and has fully resolved.

1. CHRONIC GVHD -- NEW DIAGNOSIS, MILD:
   These findings are most consistent with early chronic GVHD (cGVHD) with multiorgan involvement: skin (subtle sclerosis/thickening, bilateral forearms -- NIH skin score 1), eyes (sicca, NIH eye score 1), and mouth (mucosal erythema, reduced saliva -- NIH mouth score 1). By NIH 2014 Consensus Criteria, this is classified as mild chronic GVHD (1-3 organs involved, none with severe NIH score, no lung or esophageal involvement, no functional impairment). No overlap features of acute GVHD at this time.
   - Hold further tacrolimus taper for now; maintain current tacrolimus 0.5 mg BID with trough targeting 8-12 ng/mL.
   - Initiate topical triamcinolone 0.1% cream to bilateral dorsal forearms, apply twice daily. Counseled patient to apply after moisturizing and to limit application to areas of thickening. No systemic corticosteroids indicated at this time given mild overall severity.
   - Refer to ophthalmology for formal evaluation of sicca/dry eye syndrome. In the interim, prescribe cyclosporine ophthalmic emulsion 0.05% (Restasis) one drop in each eye twice daily for anti-inflammatory management of aqueous-deficient dry eye. Continue artificial tears as needed (preservative-free preferred given anticipated frequent use).
   - For oral cGVHD: dexamethasone oral rinse (0.5 mg/5 mL) swish and spit twice daily for buccal erythema. Encourage good oral hygiene and regular dental follow-up.
   - Systemic steroids are NOT indicated at this stage given mild NIH global severity. Will reassess at next visit.
   - Initiate formal NIH cGVHD organ-specific scoring at each subsequent visit for longitudinal tracking.
   - Continue PCP, antifungal, and antiviral prophylaxis given ongoing immunosuppression.

2. AML REMISSION SURVEILLANCE -- DAY +120:
   Leukemia remains in deep molecular remission. FLT3-ITD PCR undetectable in peripheral blood. Full donor myeloid chimerism confirmed. CBC counts within normal limits with appropriate recovery. No clinical concern for early relapse.
   - Next bone marrow biopsy with MRD assessment planned for approximately day +180 (6-month post-transplant standard surveillance).
   - Continue peripheral blood chimerism and FLT3-ITD PCR monitoring every 4-6 weeks in the interim.
   - No plans for post-transplant maintenance FLT3 inhibitor (e.g., sorafenib) at this time -- will reassess at each visit in the context of ongoing MRD status and chimerism trends.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY:
   Bilateral distal toe paresthesias, stable and possibly slightly improved. Felt to be tacrolimus-related given temporal correlation with initiation and partial improvement with dose reduction. No new neurological symptoms. Will reassess with planned tacrolimus taper once cGVHD stabilizes. No intervention needed at this time.

4. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS AND MONITORING:
   CMV PCR and EBV PCR undetectable today -- reassuring given D+/R+ CMV status. Letermovir completed at day +100 per protocol.
   - Continue acyclovir, TMP-SMX, and fluconazole as currently prescribed.
   - Repeat CMV PCR in 4 weeks given ongoing immunosuppression with tacrolimus and new cGVHD management with topical/local steroids.

5. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
   Stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH within normal limits today. Continue current dose; recheck TSH in 6 months.

6. FATIGUE AND FUNCTIONAL STATUS:
   Persistent mild fatigue consistent with the post-transplant recovery trajectory at 4 months. ECOG PS 1. She has already made remarkable functional progress, returning to part-time teaching. Encouraged to continue graded physical activity as tolerated. Referral to occupational therapy/physical therapy discussed if functional status does not continue to improve.

7. IMAGING FOLLOW-UP:
   The right lower lobe ground-glass opacity seen on day +90 CT was felt to be post-infectious. Will include chest as part of the 6-month surveillance CT planned in approximately 6 weeks (prior to day +180 biopsy).

Return to clinic in 4 weeks for CBC, metabolic panel, tacrolimus trough, CMV PCR, and reassessment of cGVHD manifestations. She was instructed to contact the transplant clinic immediately for any fever, new rash, worsening diarrhea, respiratory symptoms, or significant change in functional status.

Time spent in face-to-face discussion and care coordination: approximately 45 minutes. Complexity: high (new diagnosis of chronic GVHD, ongoing post-transplant immunosuppression management, cancer surveillance, polypharmacy review).

Electronically signed by Attending Hematology/Oncology -- Stem Cell Transplant Program
